title: 🎙️ Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
description: Today we head into the wild — the Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) plateau, river valleys, and backcountry that define the Cariboo experience. We're covering the technology helping monitor, protect, and navigate these landscapes: wildfire detection, wildlife tracking, satellite communication, and the tools keeping backcountry travellers safe and conservationists informed. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we head into the wild — the Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) plateau, river valleys, and backcountry that define the Cariboo experience. We're covering the technology helping monitor, protect, and navigate these landscapes: wildfire detection, wildlife tracking, satellite communication, and the tools keeping backcountry travellers safe and conservationists informed.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: wildfire detection tech and BC Wildfire monitoring systems, satellite/drone surveillance for Chilcotin wilderness, wildlife tracking and conservation technology, watershed sensors and salmon habitat monitoring, weather stations and climate tracking in backcountry, avalanche forecasting tech, trail mapping and GPS systems, outdoor recreation apps and connectivity, backcountry communication devices (satellite messengers, Meshtastic), flood/drought prediction systems, remote cameras for wildlife research, and how technology helps manage and protect Cariboo wild spaces. Prefer articles that bridge wilderness/conservation AND technology. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): prehistoric and ancient biology (fossils, palaeontology, deep-time creatures) with no contemporary conservation technology angle — these belong to Sunday's Science theme; AI and data centre infrastructure stories about business strategy, investment, or corporate politics rather than direct wilderness, ecosystem, or land-use impact in BC; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime news with no outdoor or conservation relevance; consumer tech reviews and financial market stories; general political and judicial news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists warn invasive Asian mantises are threatening Europe's wildlife
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Two striking Asian praying mantis species that have rapidly spread across Europe have now been officially classified as invasive, raising new concerns about their impact on native wildlife. Boosted by climate change and urban environments, these fast-breeding predators are expanding northward, where they prey on native insects, pollinators, and even small vertebrates while also reducing native mantis populations through deadly mating interactions.
items.summary: Two striking Asian praying mantis species that have rapidly spread across Europe have now been officially classified as invasive, raising new concerns about their impact on native wildlife. Boosted by climate change and urban environments, these fast-breeding predators are expanding northward,

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Heavy marijuana smoking may increase cancer risk, researchers warn
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Heavy marijuana smoking may raise the risk of lung cancer and several head and neck cancers, according to growing research, but many important questions remain unanswered. Scientists are still trying to determine how much marijuana use is enough to significantly increase cancer risk. Edibles have not been linked to lung cancer so far, while the long-term effects of vaping marijuana and secondhand marijuana smoke are still being investigated.

Scientists are still trying to determine how much marijuana use is enough to significantly increase cancer risk. Edibles have

items.title: [ScienceDaily] NASA satellites are watching Earth's newest island rise from the sea
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

A newly discovered underwater volcanic eruption north of Papua New Guinea is unfolding in one of the world's most poorly mapped ocean basins. Satellites have spotted steam plumes, ash, thermal hotspots, and huge floating pumice rafts, suggesting magma is rising surprisingly close to the surface. Scientists are now watching closely to see if the eruption creates a new island, offering a rare opportunity to observe the birth of new land as it happens.
items.summary: A newly discovered underwater volcanic eruption north of Papua New Guinea is unfolding in one of the world's most poorly mapped ocean basins. Satellites have spotted steam plumes, ash, thermal hotspots, and huge floating pumice rafts, suggesting magma is rising surprisingly close to the surface.

items.title: [New Atlas] Subaquatic on-call robot guards critical deep-sea infrastructure
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Ancient pharaohs had round-the-clock guards watching their tombs. Today, some of the world’s most critical infrastructure sits on the seabed with far less attention, mostly because adequate tech doesn't exist. Researchers at the Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU) are now developing something closer to a permanent subsea guard: an autonomous underwater robot that can live at a docking station on the seabed, leave to inspect infrastructure, return on its own, recharge, and upload data without human intervention.

Continue Reading

Category: Marine, Transport

Tags: Underwater drones, drones, Monitoring, Autonomous Vehicles

items.title: [Boing Boing] Castro's supercow got a state obituary and a marble statue
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

On a single day in January 1982, a Cuban cow named Ubre Blanca ("White Udder") produced 109.5 liters of milk — "more than four times a typical cow's production," according to Wikipedia. Guinness World Records recognized that mark, along with her 24,268.9-liter output in a single lactation period. — Read the rest

The post Castro's supercow got a state obituary and a marble statue appeared first on Boing Boing.
